Class Observation Report47
Summary Report47
Background
Monitoring and appraising teachers are central to improving schools and learning environments. If well designed, teacher appraisal and feedback systems can be used as a tool to increase teacher effectiveness and achieve better student learning outcomes. The appraisal can help to increase the focus on teaching and teachers’ professional learning. Teachers need feedback on their performance to help them identify how to improve their teaching practice and, with the support of effective school leadership, to develop schools as professional learning communities. Appraisal and feedback systems can also help to build better school organization by allowing teachers to progress in their careers and to take on new roles and responsibilities based on a solid evaluation of their performance. It is also an opportunity for recognizing and rewarding effective teaching.
Appraisal criteria refer to the standards by which teachers are judged or evaluated. The establishment of quality appraisal criteria is central to an effective teacher appraisal system. This appraisal classifies teachers’ duties into five areas, which together lay the foundation for teacher appraisal: knowledge of the subject matter; instructional competence; assessment competence; professionalism, and other duties to the school and community. Teacher Evaluation Instrument consists of four aspects; Teacher administration, Class Observation, Teacher Work Inspections, and students’ surveys. Class observation consists of four domains. The planning and preparation domain includes an understanding of the content to be taught and the pedagogy used to teach it, knowledge of the students’ backgrounds, and instruction and assessment design. The classroom environment domain addresses the teacher’s skill in creating an environment conducive to learning, including both the physical and interpersonal aspects of the environment. The instruction domain is concerned with the teacher’s skill in engaging students in learning and includes a wide range of instructional strategies that enable students to learn. Finally, the professional responsibilities domain includes reflection on teaching, communication with families, growing and developing professionally, and showing professionalism. For each component or element, a four-level rubric provides a range of performance descriptions from unsatisfactory to distinguished teaching practice.
Appraisal Cycle
Orientation
At the beginning of each academic year, the Eduversal Appraisal Team conducts a socialization program for all partner schools' management board members. The timeline of the appraisal is also informed during this program. Each school needs to ensure that all of its teachers are provided with an orientation to the Eduversal Appraisal System followed by ongoing support in understanding the rubric and its application.
Before observation, the head of each department, as the appraiser, conducts a pre-conference meeting with the teacher to ask pertinent background questions about the lesson plan and the students in the class to provide context for the upcoming observation. The overall purpose of the pre-conference is for the appraiser and teacher to mutually discuss the upcoming lesson observation, focusing on the interrelationships between planning, instruction, the learning environment, and student outcomes. It is also used to brief the teachers about the details of the appraisal schedule, the observation instruments, rubrics, and the goals to be achieved.
Data Collection (Appraisal Visit)
It is essential that administrators, support staff, and teachers share conversations concerning the instructional program and strategies for meeting the needs of individual students. One of the most effective tools for gathering data for these conversations is formal or informal classroom observation. Observations should not be conducted on the last day of instruction before any official school holiday, exam dates, or on any other day deemed inappropriate by the school district board of trustees.
Before visiting the school for appraisal, Eduversal will send a formal letter to the school’s principal, informing the details of the visit.
The observation process provides a structure through which the appraiser and teacher can discuss the specific needs of students discussed in the pre-conference meeting. It also allows the appraiser the opportunity to gather lesson evidence using the four domains of the lesson rubric, and to provide feedback through a formative appraisal report and a post-observation conference on the scheduled dates.
Additionally, curriculum staff, department chairs, and building administrators may conduct snapshot visits that provide programmatic information to classroom teachers. These visits may occur anytime and should include written feedback on the designated content snapshot form. The feedback from these visits may be considered cumulative data but must be shared with the teacher in writing through the school Edunav system.
Administration
Evidence of teachers’ administrative documents is collected before the appraisal visit, by the school management board members. The data will be checked during the visit by the appraisers. It is critical for evidence to be collected in this comprehensive manner in order to give teachers a clear picture of how to effectively design and plan for instruction.
Class Observation
The ability to model the use of new information and the teacher’s expectations for student performance is one of the most critical aspects of this dimension.
The appraisers will visit the classroom on the scheduled date and time to gather evidence through the lesson observation and input all information gathered into the appraisal application system in Edunav, consisting of four domains (Planning and Organization, Classroom Management, Instructions, and Assessment).
Teacher’s Work Inspection
Unlike the lesson observation, evidence for the teacher’s work related to professionalism and interpersonal relationships is collected throughout the year and this domain is not scored in summative form by the evaluator until after the teacher has been afforded the opportunity to present evidence until the End-of-Year Conference.
This domain should be scored by someone who regularly interacts with the educator. In this case, it is more appropriate to obtain input from the school administrators, in addition to the teacher, to score this domain.
This domain specifically addresses “how” teachers fulfill their professional roles and responsibilities, engage in ongoing review and development of their practices, and adhere to the legal and ethical requirements of the profession.
Student Satisfaction Survey
Students are given a survey to fill out at the end of each semester to give feedback about the teaching and learning process implemented by their teachers. This survey form will be shared and filled out by the students through their account in their school Edunav system.
The main purpose of having this survey is to gather feedback on the effectiveness of the learning activities given by each during the lessons. It can also be used for looking for ways to improve the teachers’ teaching strategies and relationships with the students.
Post-Appraisal Conferences
The primary purpose of the post-conference is to provide quality evidence and feedback which guides professional growth over time. A clear understanding of the descriptors in the Rubric helps both appraisers and teachers understand the expectations for performance across performance levels. This support should be provided in numerous ways by administrators and/or teacher leaders including the modeling of specific dimensions in professional development meetings, in teachers’ classrooms, and in the post-conference.
The appraisers will arrange a schedule to discuss the results of the class observation with each teacher, immediately after the observation. Also, the appraisal team will meet with the school management to inform the appraisal findings and communicate the evidence to be followed up to improve the teachers’ quality.
Publication of Final Scores
The final scores for each appraisal are shared through the school Edunav system. Based on the result, teachers should reflect on progress made and begin to draft a new personal professional goal for the following school year.
Summary Report
This summary report is the endpoint to the annual appraisal process, but performance and development priorities will be reviewed and addressed on a regular basis throughout the year in interim meetings which will take place, such as the mid-year review.
The teacher will receive an individual report as soon as practicable following the end of each appraisal period through their own account in the Edunav system.
The school will also receive its written appraisal reports at the end of the academic year.
Follow-Through and Performance Planning
The appraisal is a supportive process that will be used to inform continuing professional development. The school wishes to encourage a culture in which all teachers take responsibility for improving their teaching through appropriate professional development. Professional development will be linked to school improvement priorities and to the ongoing professional development needs and priorities of individual teachers.
Roles and Responsibilities
Eduversal
Eduversal must, before, or as soon as practicable after, the start of each appraisal period in relation to school management:
inform the school management of the standards against which the school's performance in that appraisal period will be assessed; and
set objectives for the school in respect of that period.
Eduversal must exercise its functions so as to secure that the duty in school management paragraph (1) and (2) is complied with.
Eduversal must adopt and make available to teachers employed at that school a document which sets out the appraisal process for such teachers.
As soon as practicable following the end of each appraisal period applying in relation to a teacher, Eduversal must provide the teacher with a written report of the teacher’s appraisal in respect of that appraisal period.
School Management
The school management of a school, in respect of every teacher employed at that school, before, or as soon as practicable after, the start of each appraisal period in relation to a teacher:
Inform the teacher of the standards against which the teacher’s performance in that appraisal period will be assessed; and
Set objectives for the teacher in respect of that period.
The school management must appraise the performance of every teacher employed at that school in respect of each appraisal period applying in relation to the teacher.
In making appraisal, the school management must:
Assess the teacher’s performance of their role and responsibilities during the appraisal period in question against:
The standards applicable to that teacher by virtue of appraisal framework; and
The teacher’s objectives set under appraisal framework;
Assess the teacher’s professional development needs and identify any action that should be taken to address them; and
Where relevant under the Document, include a recommendation relating to the teacher’s role
As soon as practicable following the end of each appraisal period applying in relation to a teacher, the school management must provide the teacher with a written report of the teacher’s appraisal in respect of that appraisal period.
Appraiser (Head of Department)
The roles and responsibilities of the Appraiser are:
Understand the wider perspective of the priorities and developments for the school
Ensure a sound knowledge of the role and work of the appraisee
Consult the current job description of the appraisee and relevant standards
Consult previous appraisal records (where applicable)
Gather and review appropriate evidence and ensure that all evidence has been shared with the appraisee prior to the meeting
Agree on a mutually convenient time and place
Discuss the following
Review of previous year’s objectives
The appraisee’s work over the last appraisal cycle - successes, achievements
Any factor which has had an impact on the effectiveness
Progress towards their objectives using appropriate evidence
Performance against relevant teacher standards
Impact of support and training/professional development
Agree on a recommendation role for eligible teachers
Planning future objectives
Future objectives linked to the job role, experience, school priorities, teacher standards, and future career progression
Support professional learning/development activities needed to meet objectives
Agree on evidence/success criteria and evidence which will be used to assess performance including classroom observation
Keep an accurate record of the discussion in order to complete relevant documentation
Write the appraisal statement
Monitor and review progress throughout the year towards agreed objectives, ensuring the provision of planned support and development
Appraisee (Teaching staff and non-teaching staff)
The roles and responsibilities of the Appraisee are:
Carry out a self-review reflecting on:
Your current role/job description
Key successes, achievements, challenges
Progress towards objectives
Assessment against the teacher/standards
Think about possible future objectives and training and development needs
Discuss the following
Review of previous year’s objectives
Key successes, achievement over appraisal cycle
Progress towards objectives using appropriate evidence
Performance against relevant teacher standards
Any factor which has had an impact on the effectiveness
Impact of support and training/professional development
Planning future objectives
Future objectives linked to the job role, experience, school priorities, teacher standards, and future career progression
The support professional learning/development activities needed to meet objectives
Agree on performance/success criteria and evidence which will be used to assess performance including classroom observation
Develop skills and practice related to the objectives
Participate in interim discussion to review progress
Ensure reviewers are made aware of any concerns at the time they arise about the progress of provision of support or training
Teacher Appraisal Frameworks, Instruments, and Rubrics
Appraisal Frameworks
Appraisal Instruments and Rubrics
FRAMEWORK 1 - TEACHER ADMINISTRATION
1. School academic calendar
Component must be included in school academic calendar: school events and programs, first day school, effective week, exam date, report distribution, school events, national holidays, semester holiday, end of academic year
1
(Unsatisfactory)
2
(Basic)
3
(Proficient)
4
(Distinguished)
He/she fails to show the school academic calendar.
Available, however, not all components are included.
Available and his/her document. In addition, it includes components on student orientation, effective week, semester break, report distribution, national holiday, and school holiday. However, school events and programs are not included.
Available and his/her own document. In addition, it includes all the necessary components.
2. Annual plan & syllabus
Complete component to annual plan & syllabus: 1) identity (school name, grade, subject, academic year, teacher's name), 2) learning objective(s), 3) topic and subtopic, 4) time allocation, 5) resource(s)
1
(Unsatisfactory)
2
(Basic)
3
(Proficient)
4
(Distinguished)
Document is available. It includes only 2 out of 5 required components.
Document is available. It includes 3 out of 5 required components.
Document is available. It includes 4 out of 5 required components.
Document is available. It includes all 5 required components.
3. Lesson plan submission
Lesson plan submission in Edunav system
1
(Unsatisfactory)
2
(Basic)
3
(Proficient)
4
(Distinguished)
Many lesson plans are missing or submitted significantly past the deadline (<50%).
A significant number of lesson plans are submitted, but some are missing or late (50% to <75%).
The majority of lesson plans are submitted on time, with minimal missing components (75% to <100%).
All lesson plans are submitted to Edunav by the given deadline (100%)
4. Lesson plan format
Complete component lesson plan format: 1) identity such as school name, subject name, grade, semester, topics, time allocation, teacher' name, date), 2) lesson objective(s), 3) detailed lesson activities, 4) assessment
1
(Unsatisfactory)
2
(Basic)
3
(Proficient)
4
(Distinguished)
Document is available. It includes only 1 out of 4 required components.
Document is available. It includes only 2 out of 4 required components.
Document is available. It includes 3 out of 4 required components.
Document is available. It includes all 4 required components.
5. Lesson schedule
Lesson timetable
1
(Unsatisfactory)
2
(Basic)
3
(Proficient)
4
(Distinguished)
Lesson schedule is not available, either in hard copy or digital copy.
Lesson schedule is available, either in hard copy or digital copy.
6. Lesson journal
Lesson journal in Edunav system
1
(Unsatisfactory)
2
(Basic)
3
(Proficient)
4
(Distinguished)
No record found in the session journals.
The journals are not written completely for each meeting session (<50%).
The journals are written frequently (50% to <100%).
The journals are regularly written for each session (100%).
7. Gradebook
Gradebook in Edunav system
1
(Unsatisfactory)
2
(Basic)
3
(Proficient)
4
(Distinguished)
Gradebook is unavailable or inaccessible.
Gradebook is available, but it is not frequently updated.
Gradebook is available and are updated with some regularity, but not always promptly.
Gradebook is available and updated regularly.
8. Student's attendance
Students' attendance are recorded to the Edunav system
1
(Unsatisfactory)
2
(Basic)
3
(Proficient)
4
(Distinguished)
Students' attendance records cannot be found.
Students' attendance record is rarely updated (50%).
Students' attendance record is frequently updated (50% to <100%).
Students' attendance record is regularly updated (100%).
9. Coursebook
Coursecooks are available, either in hard copy or digital copy
1
(Unsatisfactory)
2
(Basic)
3
(Proficient)
4
(Distinguished)
The teacher does not have the coursebook, either in hard copy or digital copy.
The teacher has the coursebook, either in hard copy or digital copy.
FRAMEWORK 2 - CLASS OBSERVATION
DOMAIN 1 - PLANNING AND ORGANIZATION
1. prepares lesson plans that are well laid out, sequenced, and includes timing as an integral part of the planning
1
(Unsatisfactory)
2
(Basic)
3
(Proficient)
4
(Distinguished)
Plans for learning are not well structured or sequenced.
Plans for learning are partially well structured but may not be realistic about time expectations.
Plans for learning are well structured and have a flow that allows for students that supports student learning and reflection.
Plans for learning are well structured and have a flow autonomy in the learning process.
2. writes objectives that are clear, level appropriate, and achievable
1
(Unsatisfactory)
2
(Basic)
3
(Proficient)
4
(Distinguished)
Learning objectives do not reflect grade-level standards, are unclear, or are written as activities.
Learning objectives partially reflect grade-level standards and somewhat clearly describe what students will learn.
Learning objectives mostly reflect grade-level standards, are clear and specific, and likely help students understand what and how they will learn.
Learning objectives consistently reflect challenging grade-level standards, encourage intellectual risk-taking, and are clear and specific in supporting student learning.
3. prepares content that is a good match for the objectives
1
(Unsatisfactory)
2
(Basic)
3
(Proficient)
4
(Distinguished)
The planned content shows little or no alignment with the stated learning objectives, making it unlikely students will achieve them.
The planned content has some alignment with the learning objectives, but it may not be consistently clear, fully supportive, or appropriate for achieving all stated outcomes.
The planned content is clearly and consistently aligned with the learning objectives, providing a solid foundation for students to achieve them.
The planned content is well-aligned with the learning objectives, is thoughtfully selected or designed, and strategically organized to ensure all students can effectively achieve and potentially extend beyond the objectives.
4. plans activities that are well differentiated
1
(Unsatisfactory)
2
(Basic)
3
(Proficient)
4
(Distinguished)
There is only a single approach or activity planned for students to learn the content.
There are multiple ways for students to learn the content and a variety of strategies are used with some success during the lesson.
Multiple strategies and approaches are tailored to the needs of various students to accelerate and support their success.
Lessons provide opportunities for students to engage beyond the content of the lesson; and, when appropriate, the teacher works with students to co-design learning experiences.
5. prepares instruction with opportunities for individual and/or group work
1
(Unsatisfactory)
2
(Basic)
3
(Proficient)
4
(Distinguished)
The teacher's instruction consistently lacks opportunities for individual or group work, leading to limited student engagement and interaction.
The teacher occasionally incorporates individual or group work, but the activities may not be well-structured or effectively support student learning.
The teacher regularly provides opportunities for both individual and group work, with activities strategically designed to promote collaboration, critical thinking, and active participation.
The teacher expertly integrates individual and group work into instruction, fostering a dynamic and interactive learning environment where students learn from and support one another.
6. prepares instructional materials that are adequate and usable in the setting
1
(Unsatisfactory)
2
(Basic)
3
(Proficient)
4
(Distinguished)
Tasks and activities are uninteresting to students, do not challenge them, and/or do not align to instructional outcomes.
Tasks and activities are partially aligned to the instructional outcomes and represent a modest level of intellectual challenge.
Tasks and activities are aligned with learning outcomes and provide opportunities for higher level thinking.
Tasks and activities encourage student agency and create authentic opportunities to engage with meaningful content.
7. is organised for lesson delivery and materials
1
(Unsatisfactory)
2
(Basic)
3
(Proficient)
4
(Distinguished)
Few activities and materials are sequenced and rarely provides time for lesson and lesson closure.
Most activities and materials are sequenced and sometimes provide appropriate time for lesson and lesson closure.
All activities and materials are sequenced, relevant to students, and provide appropriate time for lesson and lesson closure.
Lessons flow smoothly, connect to students' existing knowledge and real-world uses, and give them enough time to learn, reflect, and wrap up.
8. prepares assessment(s) to monitor students’ learning
1
(Unsatisfactory)
2
(Basic)
3
(Proficient)
4
(Distinguished)
There are no formative assessments planned.
Some formative assessments are planned and will give a partially accurate picture of student learning throughout the lesson.
Formative assessments will give an accurate picture of student learning and plans for lessons indicate possible adjustments based on formative assessment data.
Formative assessments provide accurate and timely information to students, allowing them to adjust course with minimal teacher guidance.
9. makes content accessible and engaging
1
(Unsatisfactory)
2
(Basic)
3
(Proficient)
4
(Distinguished)
Lessons lack consideration for what students already know or may misunderstand.
Lessons partially address what students know and include somewhat suitable teaching strategies.
Lessons use varied and appropriate teaching strategies, showing an understanding of how topics connect.
Lessons use carefully chosen strategies that encourage deep thinking, skill development, and high-level engagement.
10. makes interdisciplinary connections
1
(Unsatisfactory)
2
(Basic)
3
(Proficient)
4
(Distinguished)
Lessons don't make connections to other subjects.
Lessons mention some connections to other subjects.
Lessons show knowledge of connections between subjects and how they apply to real life.
Lessons focus on connecting subjects, emphasizing skills useful in multiple subjects, and showing how they apply to real life.
DOMAIN 2 - CLASSROOM MANAGEMENT
1. shows awareness of classroom dynamics and ensures students follow behavioral and activity guidelines
1
(Unsatisfactory)
2
(Basic)
3
(Proficient)
4
(Distinguished)
The teacher doesn't show awareness of what is going on in the classroom. Teacher doesn't encourage students to monitor their own behaviour and one another's behaviour.
The teacher is aware of classroom happenings but struggles to get students to follow rules.
The teacher is aware of classroom happenings and encourages students to monitor their own and others' behavior.
Students successfully monitor their behavior and support positive behavior in others.
2. creates an atmosphere conducive to learning
1
(Unsatisfactory)
2
(Basic)
3
(Proficient)
4
(Distinguished)
Classroom interactions are negative and inappropriate.
The classroom atmosphere is somewhat inconsistent and only occasionally supports student learning.
The classroom atmosphere is generally positive and supports student learning most of the time.
The classroom atmosphere is consistently positive, engaging, and highly supportive of all students' learning.
3. is fair in dealing with students
1
(Unsatisfactory)
2
(Basic)
3
(Proficient)
4
(Distinguished)
The teacher ignores student questions and struggles to adjust lessons.
The teacher answers some questions and attempts to adjust lessons with minimal success.
The teacher successfully accommodates student questions and adjusts instruction.
The teacher seizes on a teachable moment to enhance a lesson and/or successfully adjusts and differentiates instruction to address individual student misunderstandings.
4. manages time effectively
1
(Unsatisfactory)
2
(Basic)
3
(Proficient)
4
(Distinguished)
Non-instructional tasks and instructional tasks are not completed or completed inefficiently resulting in potential danger to students.
Non-instructional tasks are completed with some efficiency, but instructional time is lost.
Most non-instructional and instructional tasks are completed efficiently.
Non-instructional tasks are completed seamlessly with no loss of instructional time.
5. manages and utilizes learning resources effectively
1
(Unsatisfactory)
2
(Basic)
3
(Proficient)
4
(Distinguished)
Resources are not used effectively or fairly.
Resources are used somewhat effectively, but access may not be equitable.
Resources are used effectively, and all students can access them.
Resources are used equitably, with students taking initiative in accessing them.
6. manages classroom - related activities effectively, including assignments, projects, etc
1
(Unsatisfactory)
2
(Basic)
3
(Proficient)
4
(Distinguished)
Activities are poorly managed, leading to confusion and wasted time.
Activities are managed with some organization, but transitions could be smoother.
Activities are well-managed, with clear expectations and smooth transitions.
Activities are seamlessly integrated and managed, maximizing learning and engagement.
7. demonstrates effective transition from one activity to another during instruction
1
(Unsatisfactory)
2
(Basic)
3
(Proficient)
4
(Distinguished)
The lesson pace is too slow or rushed, hindering student reflection.
The lesson pace allows for some thought and reflection.
The lesson pace supports engagement and deeper learning.
Students, guided by the teacher or independently, demonstrate autonomy and effectively use reflection to solidify their understanding and new learning.
8. takes students' attendance
1
(Unsatisfactory)
2
(Basic)
3
(Proficient)
4
(Distinguished)
The teacher doesn't take student attendance.
The teacher takes attendance but doesn't record it in Edunav.
The teacher takes attendance, records it in Edunav, but doesn't follow up on absences.
The teacher takes attendance, records it in Edunav, and follows up on absences.
DOMAIN 3 - INSTRUCTIONAL PROCESS
1. arrives for lessons on time
1
(Unsatisfactory)
2
(Basic)
3
(Proficient)
4
(Distinguished)
The teacher is late for more than 5 minutes for the lesson.
The teacher is late for 1 to 5 minutes for the lesson.
The teacher arrives on time for the lesson.
The teacher arrives early for lessons and ensures a productive start.
2. welcomes/settles and check the readiness of the class appropriately
1
(Unsatisfactory)
2
(Basic)
3
(Proficient)
4
(Distinguished)
The teacher starts the lesson directly without greeting and checking the student readiness
The teacher greets students without checking student readiness.
The teacher greets students, checks their readiness, and addresses any issues.
The teacher creates a welcoming environment where students are eager to learn and prepared to participate.
3. makes objectives explicit to students at the start of the lesson
1
(Unsatisfactory)
2
(Basic)
3
(Proficient)
4
(Distinguished)
The teacher does not state the learning objectives for the lesson.
The teacher mentions the lesson's purpose but lacks clarity or detail.
The teacher clearly explains what students will learn, why it's important, and what success looks like.
Students can articulate the lesson's purpose, value, and connections to their learning and lives.
4. engages students in activities that are appropriate and meaningful
1
(Unsatisfactory)
2
(Basic)
3
(Proficient)
4
(Distinguished)
Tasks are inappropriate for students in the class, many students are not engaged in them, or they may only require recall or use a single approach.
Students are partially engaged in tasks that require thinking as opposed to only recall; some tasks have multiple correct responses or approaches.
All students engage in activities that support agency and require critical thinking tasks require high levels of intellectual engagement and students explain their thinking.
Through choices provided by the teacher or their own initiative, students modify learning tasks to make them more meaningful or challenging.
5. engages students in activities that encourage them to think and maintain student's interest
1
(Unsatisfactory)
2
(Basic)
3
(Proficient)
4
(Distinguished)
Teacher-student interactions are limited; minimal student dialogue.
The teacher encourages interaction, but only some students participate.
The teacher successfully engages students in discussions; active participation.
Students formulate questions, initiate discussions, and assume responsibility for ensuring that all voices are heard.
6. demonstrates an awareness of students’ levels of performance and take necessary action
1
(Unsatisfactory)
2
(Basic)
3
(Proficient)
4
(Distinguished)
The teacher does not recognize or ignores evidence of low engagement or lack of student understanding.
The teacher attempts to adjust learning experiences based on evidence, but is only partially successful.
The teacher effectively adjusts learning experiences based on real-time evidence and students' individual and specific needs.
Students voice their needs and take responsibility for their learning with teacher support.
7. teaches in harmony with objectives and presents correct information,
demonstrates a good grasp of the subject matter
1
(Unsatisfactory)
2
(Basic)
3
(Proficient)
4
(Distinguished)
The teacher conveys inaccurate content knowledge, leading to student confusion.
The teacher conveys accurate content knowledge but rarely integrates learning objectives with other disciplines.
The teacher conveys accurate content knowledge in multiple contexts and sometimes integrates learning objectives with other disciplines.
The teacher displays extensive content knowledge and integrates learning objectives with other disciplines and real-world experiences.
8. uses effectively a variety of teaching strategies to enhance learning
1
(Unsatisfactory)
2
(Basic)
3
(Proficient)
4
(Distinguished)
The teacher relies on a limited range of teaching strategies with minimal impact on student learning.
The teacher incorporates some variety in teaching strategies, though their effectiveness in enhancing student learning is inconsistent.
The teacher effectively uses a variety of teaching strategies that generally enhance learning for most students.
The teacher skillfully and consistently employs a wide range of diverse teaching strategies that significantly enhance learning for all students.
9. uses appropriate instructional materials in the teaching/learning environment
1
(Unsatisfactory)
2
(Basic)
3
(Proficient)
4
(Distinguished)
The teacher does not inquire about or seek out resources to support individual student needs.
The teacher seeks some additional resources and supports aligned to the individual needs of
students.
The teacher provides resources and supports aligned to their individual needs and learning differences, allowing each student to access the curriculum and meet the learning outcomes.
Resources and support provided or suggested by the teacher promote student agency and increase efficiency in utilizing resources to support their own learning and development.
10. uses appropriate questioning techniques that encourage critical thinking and deeper learning
1
(Unsatisfactory)
2
(Basic)
3
(Proficient)
4
(Distinguished)
Questions are low-level, with single correct responses.
Questions asked promote critical thinking, but with uneven success.
Open-ended questions and discussions encourage critical thinking and multiple perspectives.
Students initiate higher-order questions and extend discussions, applying critical thinking.
11. uses appropriate questioning techniques that encourage reasoning and reflection
1
(Unsatisfactory)
2
(Basic)
3
(Proficient)
4
(Distinguished)
Questions don't require students to explain their thinking.
Questions invite some explanation and reflection, but students are only partially successful.
Questions and discussions allow students to reason, reflect, and justify their thinking.
Students challenge each other's thinking, justify reasoning, and collaboratively generate new ideas.
12. gives students opportunities to respond to questions
1
(Unsatisfactory)
2
(Basic)
3
(Proficient)
4
(Distinguished)
The teacher provides minimal or no opportunities for students to respond to questions.
The teacher offers limited or infrequent opportunities for students to respond to questions, often involving only a few students.
The teacher consistently provides adequate opportunities for a variety of students to respond to questions.
The teacher skillfully creates frequent and equitable opportunities, encouraging diverse students to respond to questions thoughtfully.
13. ensures that all students participate in instructional activities
1
(Unsatisfactory)
2
(Basic)
3
(Proficient)
4
(Distinguished)
Few students participate in instructional activities, with minimal teacher effort to engage the entire class.
The teacher attempts to involve students, but participation in instructional activities is inconsistent across the class.
The teacher employs strategies that result in most students actively participating in instructional activities.
The teacher skillfully ensures active and meaningful participation from all, or nearly all, students throughout instructional activities.
14. guides students to develop concepts/master skills
1
(Unsatisfactory)
2
(Basic)
3
(Proficient)
4
(Distinguished)
The teacher brushes aside or does not address students' questions or difficulties.
The teacher attempts to incorporate students' questions and respond to their interests, but only minimally or with limited success.
The teacher incorporates students' interests and questions into learning experiences to deepen understanding and encourage curiosity and autonomy.
Students identify new learning opportunities and take the initiative to pursue them on their own, with their peers, or with the support of the teacher or others in the school and beyond.
15. presents instruction in a logical and coherent manner
1
(Unsatisfactory)
2
(Basic)
3
(Proficient)
4
(Distinguished)
Plans for learning are not well structured or sequenced.
Plans for learning are partially well structured but may not be realistic about time expectations.
Plans for learning are well structured and have a flow that allows for student learning and reflection.
Plans for learning are well structured and have a flow that supports student autonomy in the learning process.
16. delivers moral values in lesson
1
(Unsatisfactory)
2
(Basic)
3
(Proficient)
4
(Distinguished)
No explicit modeling or teaching of character habits.
Character habits are modeled or taught with uneven success.
Character habits are modeled, taught, and reinforced.
Students take initiative in implementing and reinforcing character habits.
17. ends lessons appropriately
1
(Unsatisfactory)
2
(Basic)
3
(Proficient)
4
(Distinguished)
Lesson closure doesn't include review, summary, or linking to new ideas.
Lesson closure includes only review and summary.
Lesson closure includes review, summary, and consolidating key information.
Lesson closure includes review, summary, consolidating information, and linking to new ideas and building anticipation for the next lesson.
18. uses appropriate language (body language, fluency, vocabulary, articulates correct pronunciation, intonation, and stress of words and sentences)
1
(Unsatisfactory)
2
(Basic)
3
(Proficient)
4
(Distinguished)
The teacher's communications include incorrect or imprecise use of academic language.
Academic language is generally correct but may need further explanation.
Language and vocabulary are accurate, rigorous, and support student development.
Rich and rigorous language is consistently used by the teacher and students.
19. monitor student understanding
1
(Unsatisfactory)
2
(Basic)
3
(Proficient)
4
(Distinguished)
The teacher doesn't monitor student progress or provide opportunities for self-assessment.
Attempts to monitor understanding and support self-assessment are limited.
The teacher uses questions and assessments to elicit evidence of understanding and models self-assessment strategies.
Students take initiative to monitor their understanding and make adjustments to their learning.
20. provides timely, constructive feedback
1
(Unsatisfactory)
2
(Basic)
3
(Proficient)
4
(Distinguished)
Students receive no feedback, or feedback is global and infrequent.
Feedback is provided but may be unclear or not focused on improvement.
Students receive timely, constructive feedback that advances learning.
Students use feedback from multiple sources to make decisions and take responsibility for their learning.
21. is determined and persistent
1
(Unsatisfactory)
2
(Basic)
3
(Proficient)
4
(Distinguished)
The teacher shows little responsibility for student learning or is uncertain how to assist students.
The teacher conveys responsibility but may lack certainty in responding to student difficulties.
The teacher persists in seeking alternative approaches when students struggle.
Students demonstrate determination and persistence, seeking and receiving support.
DOMAIN 4 - ASSESSMENT
1. communicates clear criteria/standards for assessment to students
1
(Unsatisfactory)
2
(Basic)
3
(Proficient)
4
(Distinguished)
The teacher does not share assessment criteria, describe what high-quality work looks like, or explain how students will know they are successful.
The teacher provides some information about how students will be assessed, but it is incomplete or unclear.
The teacher clearly explains assessment criteria and what constitutes high-quality work, enabling students to self-assess.
The teacher actively involves students in setting ambitious standards for success and ensures those who support students understand how progress is assessed.
2. uses appropriate assessment activities to monitor student performance
1
(Unsatisfactory)
2
(Basic)
3
(Proficient)
4
(Distinguished)
The teacher does not reflect on the lesson or draws inaccurate conclusions about its success.
The teacher has a limited understanding of the lesson's effectiveness and its impact on student learning.
The teacher uses evidence from the lesson, including student work, to assess the effectiveness of instruction.
The teacher uses multiple sources of evidence to analyze the lesson's impact on student learning and development.
3. designs assessment exercises at the appropriate level and uses appropriate language, and are aligned with the learning objectives
1
(Unsatisfactory)
2
(Basic)
3
(Proficient)
4
(Distinguished)
The teacher's assessment is not aligned with the learning objectives and the students' level, and uses inappropriate language.
The teacher's assessment considers the students' level and uses appropriate language but is not aligned with the learning objectives.
The teacher's assessment is aligned with the learning objectives, the students' level, and uses appropriate language.
The teacher's assessment is aligned with the learning objectives, considers the students' level, uses appropriate language, and encourages critical thinking.
4. maintains accurate records of students’ performance in Edunav
1
(Unsatisfactory)
2
(Basic)
3
(Proficient)
4
(Distinguished)
The teacher never uses Edunav to track student learning.
The teacher rarely uses Edunav to track student learning.
The teacher often uses Edunav to track student learning but is inconsistent.
The teacher consistently uses Edunav to track student learning.
5. frequently monitors each student’s progress
1
(Unsatisfactory)
2
(Basic)
3
(Proficient)
4
(Distinguished)
The teacher does not adequately track student progress, or the approach is not focused on mastery or aligned to goals.
The teacher tracks student progress toward mastery, but it is not comprehensive or may not be useful to students and those who support them.
The teacher tracks students' progress toward mastery and communicates understandable and useful information to students and those who support them.
Students are consistently aware of their own progress toward mastery of their goals; they are able to identify, reflect on, and discuss their strengths and needs based on clear evidence of progress.
6. takes appropriate action based on results of assessments
1
(Unsatisfactory)
2
(Basic)
3
(Proficient)
4
(Distinguished)
The teacher does not plan for or consider opportunities to improve practice.
Based on reflection, the teacher has some plans for future action that will better support student learning and development.
Based on reflection, the teacher considers multiple paths forward, communicates a commitment to the success of students, and plans, practices, and implements improvements.
The teacher focuses on the best course of action for student success, perseveres through challenges, implements new strategies, and continuously reflects on the results.
FRAMEWORK 3 - TEACHER’S WORK INSPECTION
1. arrives for work on time
1
(Unsatisfactory)
2
(Basic)
3
(Proficient)
4
(Distinguished)
The teacher arrives late for work most of the time. (Less than 90% on time)
The teacher sometimes arrives late for work. (90%-94% on time)
The teacher arrives for work on time most of the time. (95-99% on time)
The teacher always arrives for work on time. (100% on time)
2. performs task well and submits required information (reports, data, etc) on time
1
(Unsatisfactory)
2
(Basic)
3
(Proficient)
4
(Distinguished)
The teacher never responds or submits any information (reports, data, etc) asked by the school management.
The teacher submits incomplete information (reports, data, etc) asked by the school management.
The teacher submits complete information (reports, data, etc) asked by the school management, but not on time.
The teacher submits complete information (reports, data, etc) asked by the school management on time.
3. seeks opportunities for his/her professional development
1
(Unsatisfactory)
2
(Basic)
3
(Proficient)
4
(Distinguished)
The teacher demonstrates little or no interest in professional learning and takes no initiative to improve.
The teacher engages in recommended and available professional learning opportunities and takes some initiative in seeking additional opportunities for professional growth.
The teacher regularly seeks out and identifies opportunities to grow and learn.
The teacher regularly seeks out and identifies opportunities to grow and learn and actively implements them by taking the leadership role at school and directing their own and/or others' learning.
4. participates in professional development
1
(Unsatisfactory)
2
(Basic)
3
(Proficient)
4
(Distinguished)
The teacher does not stay current on content and pedagogical knowledge.
The teacher engages in activities to strengthen content and pedagogical knowledge.
The teacher takes initiative in deepening their content knowledge and understanding of the curriculum.
The teacher is an expert and leader in their knowledge of content, pedagogy, and curriculum.
5. demonstrates leadership skills in the performance of duties
1
(Unsatisfactory)
2
(Basic)
3
(Proficient)
4
(Distinguished)
The teacher resists discussing feedback on their practice or ignores feedback that is given.
The teacher accepts and acts on feedback given by colleagues.
The teacher invites feedback from colleagues, plays an active role in the analysis of practice, and works collaboratively to understand and act on feedback.
The teacher takes a leadership role in the school in supporting a culture of growth and modeling a process for seeking, providing, and acting on feedback.
6. contributes to the life of the school including co-curricular activities
1
(Unsatisfactory)
2
(Basic)
3
(Proficient)
4
(Distinguished)
The teacher avoids involvement in school events, projects, and initiatives.
The teacher participates in school events, projects, and initiatives as expected.
The teacher makes a substantial contribution to school events, projects, and initiatives.
The teacher takes a leadership role in events, projects. and initiatives that contribute to the overall success of the school.
7. adheres to the code of ethics
1
(Unsatisfactory)
2
(Basic)
3
(Proficient)
4
(Distinguished)
The teacher fails to meet the Code of Ethics and meets only few professional standards (e.g., attendance, professional appearance, and behaviors)
The teacher behaves in accordance with the Code of Ethics and meets most professional standards (e.g., attendance, professional appearance, and behaviors).
The teacher behaves in accordance with the Code of Ethics and meets all professional standards (e.g., attendance, professional appearance, and behaviors).
The teacher behaves in accordance with the Code of Ethics and models all professional standards (e.g., attendance, professional appearance, and behaviors) across the campus for educators and students.
8. encourages students to respect the worth and dignity of others
1
(Unsatisfactory)
2
(Basic)
3
(Proficient)
4
(Distinguished)
The teacher avoids contributing and getting involved in taking actions to encourage students to respect the worth and dignity of others.
When reprimanded, the teacher takes actions in encouraging students to respect the worth and dignity of others.
The teacher regularly takes actions in encouraging students to respect the worth and dignity of others.
The teacher takes on leadership roles in promoting actions or activities to encourage students to respect the worth and dignity of others.
9. contributes to school community and is co-operative and works well with staff members
1
(Unsatisfactory)
2
(Basic)
3
(Proficient)
4
(Distinguished)
The teacher's relationships with colleagues are characterized by negativity or combativeness.
The teacher has cordial relationships with colleagues but may not have established many trusting or collaborative relationships with colleagues.
The teacher has supportive and collaborative relationships with colleagues that are built on mutual respect and trust.
The teacher actively builds relational trust with colleagues and models collaboration focused on student success.
10. maintains effective and positive communication with the school stakeholders
1
(Unsatisfactory)
2
(Basic)
3
(Proficient)
4
(Distinguished)
The teacher is frequently unclear and dishonest in communication, showing little cultural sensitivity.
The teacher demonstrates relatively good communication with students, colleagues, and staff.
The teacher demonstrates cultural sensitivity, communicates well, and upholds good relational standards.
The teacher demonstrates cultural sensitivity, communicates well, upholds good relational standards, and actively seeks out feedback from stakeholders and uses it to improve performance.
FRAMEWORK 4 - STUDENT SURVEY
No.
Instruments
Strongly Disagree
Disagree
Agree
Strongly Agree
1
gave assignments that were helpful in understanding the subject better.
2
took an action for late submission
3
gave an opportunity for students to ask
4
created comfortable learning environment
5
gave an opportunity for students to give feedback on the course
6
confidence in delivering the course
7
answered students' questions clearly
8
briefly summarized the previous lecture at the beginning of each class
9
never said, "I have explained the topic. It is your problem if you have not understood it."
10
took very interactive sessions
11
offered helpful suggestions regarding recommended learning resources (book/websites), project, and evaluation
12
clearly explained the concept and processes related to topic
13
explained questions and their answers once exams were finished
14
gave feedback to student's assignments
15
clearly explained the grading criteria to students
16
offered help to students whenever in need
17
offered practical (experiment) of the subject
Teacher Appraisal Description
Purpose
This handbook sets out the framework for a clear and consistent assessment of the overall performance of teachers and for supporting their development within the context of the school’s plan for improving educational provision and performance, and the standards expected of teachers (explained in the given framework and rubrics). It also sets out the arrangements that will apply when teachers fall below the levels of competence that are expected of them.
Appraisal
The appraisal will be a supportive and developmental process designed to ensure all teachers have the skills and support they need to provide an outstanding level of education. At its core is the ongoing development of professional practice and dialogue between colleagues that identifies strengths and areas for development, with clear targets linking the whole school and individual priorities.
Eduversal is committed to ensuring the consistency of treatment and fairness within the operation of the Appraisal Process. The purpose of this policy is to set out the parameters and supporting framework to ensure this is the case.
Eduversal is also committed to providing high-quality professional development in order to support the ongoing development of staff, and this is a central part and outcome of the appraisal process.
The appraisal period
The appraisal period will run for one academic year.
Teachers who are employed on a fixed-term contract of less than one year will have their performance managed in accordance with the principles underpinning this handbook. The length of the period will be determined by the duration of their contract. However, the formal school appraisal visits (lesson observation) will be carried out in a week according to specified dates for each school.
During the appraisal visit, teachers will receive appraisal feedback on a minimum of one announced 35-40 minutes formal observation. By mutual consent of the teacher and the appraiser, the required minimum of 35-40 minutes of observation may be conducted in shorter time segments. However, the time segments must aggregate to at least 35 minutes. A formative appraisal report should be shared with the teacher after the observation. Additionally, teachers will receive feedback on a minimum of an unannounced 10-15 minute informal observation. Both the formal and the informal observations must be conducted by the assigned appraiser in person in a face-to-face classroom setting.
Two conferences will be held between the teacher and the appraiser regarding the formal
observation: a pre-conference prior to the formal observation and a post-conference following the formal observation. The overall purpose of the pre-conference is for the appraiser and teacher to mutually discuss the upcoming lesson observation with a focus on the interrelationships between planning, instruction, learning environment and student outcomes. The purpose of the post conference is to provide quality evidence and feedback which guides professional growth over time.
Appointing appraisers
The appraiser has a vital role in assessing teaching proficiency and ensuring that teachers and administrators derive reliable and meaningful information from the teacher evaluation process. By providing evidence-based feedback, appraisers help teachers and administrators make decisions that support efforts to improve instructional quality and student performance.
The teacher appraisal process requires at least one appraiser. The teacher's supervisor shall conduct the teacher's appraisal and must hold a superintendent, mid-management (principal), or supervisor certification, and have at least three years of early years, elementary, or secondary teaching experience.
Reviewing performance
Eduversal believes that observation of classroom practice and other responsibilities is important both as a way of assessing teachers’ performance in order to identify any particular strengths and areas for development they may have and of gaining useful information which can inform school improvement more generally. All observations will be carried out in a supportive fashion.
Teachers’ performance will be regularly observed but the amount and type of classroom observation will depend on the individual circumstances of the teacher and the overall needs of the school. In addition to formal observation, school leaders with responsibility for teaching standards may appraise the teacher’s lesson depending on school circumstances.
Teachers (including the head teacher) who have responsibilities outside the classroom should also expect to have their performance of those responsibilities observed and assessed.
Development and support
The appraisal is a supportive process that will be used to inform continuing professional development. Eduversal wishes to encourage a culture in which all teachers take responsibility for improving their teaching through appropriate professional development. Professional development will be linked to school improvement priorities and to the ongoing professional development needs and priorities of individual teachers.
Feedback
Teachers will receive constructive feedback on their performance throughout the year and as soon as practicable after observation has taken place or other evidence has come to light. Feedback will highlight particular areas of strength as well as any areas that need attention. Where there are concerns about any aspects of the teacher’s performance the appraiser will meet the teacher formally to:
give clear feedback to the teacher about the nature and seriousness of the concerns;
give the teacher the opportunity to comment and discuss the concerns;
agree on any support (e.g. coaching, mentoring, structured observations), that will be provided to help address those specific concerns;
make clear how, and by when, the appraiser will review progress;
explain the implications and process if no – or insufficient – improvement is made.
When progress is reviewed, if the appraiser is satisfied that the teacher has made, or is making, sufficient improvement, the appraisal process will continue as normal, with any remaining issues continuing to be addressed through that process.
Report
Ratings
Each teacher will be given a rating for each instrument observed, based on the following calculation:
Rating
Score
Distinguished
4
Proficient
3
Basic
2
Unsatisfactory
1
Summary Report
No.
Domain
Weight
1.
Administrative
20%
2.
Classroom Observation:
60%
2.a. Planning and Organization
2.b. Classroom Management
2.c. Instructional Process
2.d. Assessment
3.
Work Inspection
20%
Score and Predicate
Predicates are determined by converting the final scores from the appraisal report, which are calculated based on the given percentages for each framework.
Final Score
Predicate
86 - 100
A
66 - 85
B
51 - 65
C
36 - 50
D
0 - 35
F
Student Satisfaction Survey Result
Schools and teachers will be provided the result of the survey through their account in Edunav Appraisal platform and each teacher needs to create action plans accordingly.
Class Observation Report
The teacher’s performance report during the appraisal is provided after the post-appraisal conferences. The teacher will receive an individual report as soon as practicable following the end of each appraisal period through their own account in the Edunav Appraisal system.
Summary Report
The summary report is provided for each teacher after the post appraisal conferences.
The appraisal summary report will include:
an assessment of the teacher’s performance of their role and responsibilities against their targets and the relevant standards;
an assessment of the teacher’s training and development needs and identification of any action that should be taken to address them;
a recommendation on role where that is relevant;
a recommendation on the programs, training, and workshops for the teacher professional development.
